figures.caption: Figure 1: A visual question answering example generated by MM-Eureka-7B (Meng et al., 2025), where the hallucinated content is highlighted in red.
Table 1: Performance comparison of RC-DPO with baseline methods on the Object HalBench. CS and CI denote CHAIRS and CHAIRI respectively, where lower values indicate fewer hallucinations.
Table 3: Comparison of RC-DPO with baseline methods on the POPE benchmark.
Figure 10: Prompt used to verify the quality of CoT-answer trajectories during MCTS sampling.
Table 2: Comparison of RC-DPO with baseline methods on the POPE benchmark.

Figure 4: Radar chart of the GPT-4 assisted evaluation results. We report the Sentence-level Hallucination Ratio (SHR), Word-level Hallucination Ratio (WHR), 1&2-gram scores, and Words Per Image (WPI). A larger radar area indicates better overall performance.
Figure 11: Visualization results comparing our RC-DPO and other methods on MM-Eureka-7B. Hallucinations are marked in red.

Figure 2: Motivating observations for RC-DPO. (a) Loss-ratio curves of CoT and answer segments during OPADPO training. (b) Segment-level CHAIRS scores of the original model, OPA-DPO, and the answer-only variant OPA-DPOAns. (c) Conditional distribution between CoT CHAIRi scores and answer CHAIRi scores.
Table 6: Performance comparison of RC-DPO and baseline methods on the MME benchmark with MM-Eureka.

Figure 5: Ablation studies of RC-DPO. (a) Effects of different components on CHAIRS . (b) Sensitivity analysis of the reasoning-conditioned loss weight λ. (c) Sensitivity analysis of the pruning rate for negative CoT construction.

Figure 3: Overview of RC-DPO. We first construct SFT data to align the CoT format. Then, we construct positive and negative reasoning samples through MCTS sampling and attention-guided token pruning, respectively. Finally, we optimize the policy model with both response-level and reasoning-conditioned preference objectives.
Table 5: Comparison of RC-DPO with baseline methods on the AMBER benchmark.
Figure 9: Few-shot prompt used to generate well-structured reasoning trajectories for MCTS-based positive sample construction. Parts of the demonstrations are omitted due to space limitations.
Table 4: Comparison of RC-DPO with baseline methods on the MMHal-Bench.

Figure 6: Segment-level CHAIRS results on Object HalBench. We compare Vanilla, OPA-DPO, and RC-DPO across three MLRMs, and report CHAIRS scores for the full response, CoT, and final answer separately.
